speaker: interviewer
text: 1994. So we were talking about how terrible Bergen-Belsen was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm. Mm hm. Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were talking just before the liberation, about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so, it was wintertime. And there were not enough healthy people to take away the deaths. So that-- that hill of deaths, of corpses, in front of our barracks grow every day, higher and higher. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] People died like flies, all over. In the last days, we didn't have food. We didn't have water. We didn't have all the time water-- to wash our self. The-- the-- the toilets were overflow. We stepped, actually, in shit. Well, we had the hats, I told you about it-- on the walkway, in the barrack. By each hat there was a can-- conserve can, you see. Because some people-- most of the people-- had diarrhea. They didn't have time to get out. So they kept the can right over there. It was-- we had a cow where we lived, in that small town. That cow was much, much better treated and [INAUDIBLE], you see. There was dirt all over. And the lice-- the lice-- we went out with my friend, with [? Bashi, ?] and we just-- just to try to-- to-- to-- to air that-- that blanket of ours, you know, to scatter the-- the-- the lice off. The lice was terrible, terrible, terrible, terrible. They ate us up, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the liberation in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were telling, days before, that we are going to be liberated, we are going to be-- I saw already the white flags-- I mean, white clothes, in-- hanging here and there. I didn't understand that time what that means. I find that out later, that, OK, we are ready to receive them. I was very sick. I was just-- just coping with the typhus, when I heard some noises outside-- very unusual noises. I managed to get up. And somehow, I went out, you see. And I saw I big hill of human bodies, of in-- of the inmates, you know, the-- and in the middle of it, there was a flag, the-- the-- the British, the British flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- that was a British tank covered-- they-- they all-- they all climbed that-- that tank. And they-- they were yelling. And of course, they were very happy. I-- I didn't feel anything-- absolute nothing. I didn't-- I wasn't happy. I wasn't crying. I-- I just, OK, we are liberated. But it was like nothing. I don't know. We became so dumb, you know, so-- not dumb. No, that's the word. Numb, numb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Numb! Thank you. And I was also very sick. I was half way, you know, between-- I think between death and life. I just-- I just hold my hand to the wall. And I-- I just grab-- grab myself, you know. That-- that's the word. I went inside. And I lay on the floor. And that was it. And that was the end of it. I want to add to that, it-- it also took me a while to grab, to understand, what's going on. That, a couple of days later, of course, they-- they-- they put on right away the loud speakers. And they told in all languages that we are liberated. And they-- later they brought food. They took the sick people in the hospitals. But-- but Bergen-Belsen was-- it cannot be described. It doesn't matter how many book you read. It doesn't matter how many movies you saw. Nothing-- nothing can you give you the true pictures. Even I, who survived, who was there, cannot express, cannot-- cannot tell you exactly what it was. One had to live through. There were a few lucky who lived through that. Over there, that were the necessary thing. And I think that's the end of my life, too. I lost my mother, of course, and my father, and I lost Moisha, and [? Raisi, ?] and all my uncles. I had seven aunts and uncles. I had I don't know how many. Because there were big families that time. I don't know how many cousins. I tried I once to figure out. I always forget the number. It really doesn't matter one more or less. Maybe it matters for that one. I lost all my friends, my neighbors. I went back to my hometown, to that little town where I growed up. You know, the whole town looked liked a cemetery. Nobody was there. Nobody was there, the families. And I had some young friends who got married before me. Because they were very religious. But they didn't survive. Because they had the children. You see, who had children didn't survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what bothers me most-- those who dare to tell that wasn't a Holocaust. How can they tell such a-- that bothers me more than anything else, if I hear that. Sometimes I hear it on television. I-- I-- am aghast. You know, what do they think? Where are those people? Where are all those-- where is [? Warsaw, ?] where millions of people, even-- the whole Poland. You see, where are those people? We were-- where are the mothers? Where are the children? Where is everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one of my uncles survived-- not one of my aunts. I had seven or eight. Nobody. And of course, the children. And even the grownups-- you had to be very strong. You had to be strong, bodily. You had to be strong--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mentally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mentally, mentally. And-- and psychologically, you see, to survive that. That's-- that's-- especially to survive Bergen-Belsen, to survive a typhus, you know. Because-- because, most of them died. But who survived had-- was also survived the-- the sickness. Bergen-Belsen is one of the worst, one of the worst. You see, in-- in Auschwitz they-- they cremated. They gassed the people. And in Bergen-Belsen they died-- SS men, just-- just by treating them like-- not like animals. Because a dog is treated today like a princess in some places. I knew dogs, they have gold chain. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life after the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I-- I stood-- I stood there for a couple of months, after the liberation. Because nobody took care. Nobody thought about it, to bringing us home. I don't know why. But we were-- we were-- we were moved to better place. We-- we actually was in a place where [GERMAN] lived before. So, of course, we have rooms. We have each our beds. We have food. That's-- we had, more or less-- we were all right. But then we made a group. And we-- I had that written already in one of my stories, when we-- we came home. I came to Satmar first. That wasn't so easy. We-- we came on the top of the trains, and-- and like that, you see. Because it was after the war. It-- it was-- you couldn't get a ticket. You couldn't get a place. You couldn't get nothing. And from Satmar, I find out that I have a brother. In Satmar there was nobody anymore. In Kolozsvar-- Cluj-- which is a big city. He lives there. I-- I start-- I wanted to go to my sister, to Bucharest. I know she's all right. But I stopped in that city, Cluj-- Klausenburg. And I find my-- I found my brother. And the brother told me, look, my elder brother and my younger brother is by my sister. How many do you want to be? You stay here with me. And I will have. You will have, which was-- which was very nice. Well, we were yeah, very good sisters. And we had a beautiful family. I can tell that. And, well, I stood there. Then I went to visit my sister, of course. And I stood by my sister for a couple of months. I came back. I was by my brother. My brother was a friend to my husband. You know. And he came in one day, looking for my brothers. I looked at him. And I liked him. You know, I did-- thought myself, oh, this would be-- I would like him for my husband. That was a-- I told that to him a good many today. [INAUDIBLE]. So-- so I was-- I decided that in 10 minutes. But it took him about 10 months to came to the same decision. Anyway, we got married. It was hard time, in the beginning-- we didn't have nothing, just nothing, absolute. Because, we came-- he came home naked. I came home naked. We had our house, actually. But after the war, there was such an inflation, nothing was worth anything. But my brother sold that house. And he had a children wagon business, my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what my husband did, before the communists came. Then he-- then they nationalized it. So I got married. It took-- it took awhile until I settled. I was-- I was upset for awhile. I was very lonely. I was very alone. I-- I couldn't find my place, somehow. But-- but I was OK. I gained-- for double. I became so fat that when my sister saw me, she says, what happening to you? She was expecting a skinny woman. I got-- meanwhile, I was a couple of months with my brother. And I ate, day and night. But we were so hungry, I don't know how is that-- we are constantly-- when we had lot to eat, we ate all kinds. It didn't matter what-- whatever we had. It wasn't too much food. But that how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married, with nothing-- absolute. We had a hard time getting a place where to live. I got pregnant very fast, which I didn't want to. But it happened anyway. My daughter was-- I was married in '47-- 1947, February. Pretty soon, it's going to be 50 years. And my daughter-- that was February. My daughter was born in the same year, in December 25th, 10 months after. It wouldn't matter if it were would be earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I forgot I am on the camera. Now, anyway. So right, we liked each other. It was quite a nice life. And in the beginning, I-- somehow I put aside the-- the past. I was occupied with my family life, with my children. You see, I had my husband in the business. We had the [GERMAN], little factory, a shop. And I didn't talk too much about the concentration camp. And I didn't talk to my children, either, until they were small. Somehow, I-- I didn't know what to do. I didn't know how to tell them. I-- I don't know. I-- I didn't want to hurt them. And I thought they wouldn't understand. I-- I-- really, I wasn't-- I had a problem with them. One day, my daughter came home from the school. She says, I have to write about some kind of a composition about grandmother. Well, I said, you know, you don't have a grandmother. But I have to write it. Leah, I can't help you. What should I do with you? And you know what? I-- I made an article for her. And I-- I described her grandmother as my mother would be, which I-- which I never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But life went on. Life went on. We had the children. And it wasn't a-- a picnic. And we lived under the communist rule for 16, 17 years, see. There was no religion. There was very little Jewish life. You can do it in your own home. You-- you could do whatever you wanted between the closed doors. But-- but you hide the two Shabbos candles, you see, from certain eyes. And, of course, children went to school Saturday. And my husband worked Saturday. Because it was a six-day working day-- working week, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter went to music school. Because somehow it was discovered that she is very good in music. And when we-- when we ask for our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] visa-- not a visa. When we asked for a-- what should I call it? That we wanted to go-- wanted to leave, wanted to go to Israel, she was-- she was just thrown out from music school, because of that. Can you believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all right, that's-- that's not a tragedy. I-- I'm just mentioning it. And we-- we waited for that-- for that permission-- a permit, actually-- to leave Romania, 13 years-- 13 years. My son was 13. My daughter was 17 when we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the permit was to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To come to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- they didn't let-- to Israel. But we came here, because I had here a sister and two brothers. And they wanted us only here-- my brother from Israel wanted us to Israel. And we got to the highest. They said, you have to decide. Because everybody wants-- where to go? Everybody wants you. You want to go to Israel? You want to go-- but here-- my sister, my brother-- they wrote letters, and called. Come here. You have always time to go to Israel. Now the doors are open for you. You can come in. You have always time to change your mind and go to Israel. And you know, you wouldn't believe it. I came to America with the thought that we'll go to Israel, which, of course, never happened. Yeah, well, that change our life radically, of course. Well, life Romania-- well, it was it was life by the communist. We missed a lot of things. We were-- we were-- we were brainwashed, actually. You know, there is a saying-- if the worm eats itself in the horseradish, it thinks there is no better than that. I think we we lived with that thought. Because they had paper. They had theater. They had movies. They had television-- everything about their ideology. So we really-- we really thought maybe that's how it is. The-- the reason-- we wanted to be with our family, and we wanted to bring up our children in a Jewish environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to the United States, was it hard to settle here, to get-- you know, to work, to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. I would say that long-- God bless America. But-- but it was very hard to-- to get accustomed-- accustomed. It was very hard to-- to get over the-- the culture shock. That was very, very, very hard-- terrible hard, terrible hard. Because you came here. And you feel like-- like stupid, you see? I felt like I fell back in the village. Because, you know, the language and everything is so different. It's-- it's like you would come to another planet. So different-- the houses, the smell, the streets, the people, the language, the way how they behave, the-- their manners, their dresses, their-- everything. Everything is different. It's very, very, very, very hard. It's very hard to get used. I thought that's going to be easy. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I missed my whole life-- I-- I don't want to left, I thought. Because it's very, very important. I always missed a grave. I always felt like it would be so nice to-- to go to a cemetery, to say a couple of words, to complain, or to-- to be happy. Look, I have a child. I have a boy. I have a girl that does that. Just-- not because I didn't-- I never believed that they will listen, or they will answer. But it will be nice for me. I always had that desire. Well, I-- I lost a sister here. Now I have her grave. I lost a brother. [CRYING] But-- but to think about the Holocaust, it came back, actually, when I stopped to work. Because, here I worked, you see. I didn't work in [INAUDIBLE] communist here. I worked. I worked very hard. I-- I-- I'm not complaining. I wanted to work. I like to work. I had to work. It's-- it's OK. We are created for work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what I wanted to say-- you know, what was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were speaking about acclimating here, to being here, and starting to talk about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, you see, I was very busy. I had a very, very busy life here. I had the two kids. I had the house. I did everything by myself-- everything. And [SIGHS] all the family matters, all the letters, all-- everything, everything. So I was very, very occupied. I think I didn't have room for that in my mind. And then later-- in the later years, I worked-- I work only four days a week. I started to write, you see. And then-- then little-- I write that poem. You read it-- about my mother. Little by little, everything came alive. I-- I didn't forget almost anything-- maybe little things. But everything which is important, I remember so well, so vividly. So, I describe my old home, my-- how we leaved-- everything that. But this is just an interview. You know, I can't tell everything. I have written about everything, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- when you were with friends, did you talk about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Some friends just refused to talk about it. But I have one friend who I met accidentally. I don't know what was it-- a wedding or something. We sat in the same table. And she puts her-- she points her finger to me. 'You-- you were in here, in here?' I said, 'yes, I been.' She says, 'I-- I know you. I've been in the same-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. I've been in the same block, in a different room.' Yeah, I never-- I-- I couldn't remember her. We never-- we never talked. But she says, 'you know, what? We came in the same-- in the same-- freight train--'

speaker: interviewer
text: Transport, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She knows I was there. She says, 'we came with the same group to [NON-ENGLISH].' She knew everything-- I don't know what was about me. I have to ask her once, what was about me what she-- she knew me the whole time. She recognized me after so many years. And we became very friendly here. But with her, we talk about the things. I-- I gave everything that I wrote about it. I asked her if she wants to do that. Says she doesn't know English. She doesn't-- she doesn't feel like-- she's a very [? Hasidic. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you have gone to schools to speak about the Holocaust. Is that important to you? Is it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is very, very important for me. I cannot tell you how much it is. One of the kids who-- I got letters from the children-- writes me that, 'you are the voice for all those who cannot speak.' He just pointed to the-- the-- the topic, you know. This is-- this is a-- I don't know why. I just feel an obligation. I-- because with-- because it-- I'm revolved by hearing that-- that Holocaust didn't happen. I feel very strongly that not only we-- everybody should do the same thing. I-- I saw what-- what impression it made on the children. You know, they didn't know. They-- they were changed. One of-- one of those children-- she-- she heard me-- it's a boy. He's occupied with that. He reads everything about it. I made such an impression. I feel that everybody-- the whole world-- the whole world-- I was in a trip. I met a woman from South Africa, you know. And I sent her. She wanted to know everything. She was in Auschwitz, actually, when I was. So some people don't know anything about it. People don't know. They have to know. The whole world has to know that-- that-- this is-- I think it's-- it's very, very important that it-- that they should know what happened to innocent people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because some people made comparison. Look what's happening today in Yugoslavia, you know. Look what-- what happened to [INAUDIBLE]. But-- but that was a war. Their people were fighting each other. But we were just plain innocent, religious people, you see. Why did they deserve such a terrible death? You see. Children-- a million children-- innocent children. Whom did they hurt? No, this has to be told. This has to be told. This has to be-- I think this is a wonderful idea from-- what's his name? Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spielberg. Wonderful. Wonderful. I congratulate him. Wonderful. It's a very important-- very important task. Yeah, I-- Id somehow I feel-- I feel like it's my duty. Because some people tell, why do-- I said, this has to told! It has to-- I don't want to talk about it. What do you mean, don't want to talk about it? Look what happened to all those. You know, it's not only those who were cremated. But there were millions of people who suffered through a whole year and they died three days before liberation. My cousin-- three of them, before the-- because that was too late, already. They came too late, yeah. Suffered through a whole year. No, this has to be told. Everybody has to know that. I know there are many people-- many people, they don't know. But you see, it's 50 years-- two generation were brought up, almost, right, since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, there was children in school who never heard about that. And they were 14 years old. And I went to another school, where they didn't know absolute nothing. Because this teacher prepared the children. And so they have an idea. No. They-- they have to know. They have-- one-- one child writes me that, we-- we learned about the Second World War, about Hitler, how he occupied one country after the other. But, they didn't mention the Holocaust. You see? And schools would be very important. Because we can't do-- we don't-- can't do that much. And we are not all over. And we are, every day, less and less, you see? I-- many people died around me-- many, many already. You see, we are-- the years are passing by and--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do that. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll do it. You'll do fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Joseph. We were married 47 years ago. We're happy. And I'm glad he's near me at-- [INAUDIBLE]. OK? Yes. And this is a poem I'm going to recite, which I wrote about 10 years ago. Actually, I wrote it in Hungarian. And then, after awhile, I felt I can do it in English. I translated it. And this is how it sounds. It's very dear to me. Remembrance-- it is [NON-ENGLISH]. I ponder. I remember. Now I am surrounded with all those who were so dear to me-- my mother, my father, sisters, brothers, and so many others torn away from me by hands of murders. I see them now, as they were then. The dead do not grow old. So they remain young. My mother is in my mind most of the time, because the wound never healed, what she left behind. My thoughts take me back to Auschwitz. We are holding my mother's arm. We were chased to the infamous gate. A German officer stood in front of us. Somewhere, in his country, he must have been a physician. But as he's stood there, in his uniform-- firm, erect-- he was playing God, distributing life and death. He looked at us. His decision was swift. In directing me to the right, my mother to the left, to that fatal road, where there was no way back.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tall chimney was smoking at a distance. That was it. What was it? We could not comprehend. Our future was covered from us by a blessed hand. Amid shouting, howling, chaos, we had to move on. But I turn back quickly. And I meet-- I meet my mother's gaze. By some miracle, she stood there, looking after me in despair.

speaker: interviewee
text: In her deeply sad eyes, I could see all the Jewish mothers' pain and sorrow. The 2,000-year old Jewish tragedy, the love which radiated toward me from her dear eyes, give me strength and courage through my life. | [NON-ENGLISH] candles are sadly flickering. Or perhaps they are, too, crying. I am longing for a cemetery, to place some flowers at my mother's grave. Oh, those criminals-- deprived even from that, I do not have a grave where to bend my head. But I have with me my mother's last look, which is blurred now by the crematorium smoke. I believe a white cloud descended, lifted her up gently, and carried her to the road, which leads to Heaven. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So? We're going to do the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your turn. This is me at Satmar, in 1943. This is my father when he was about 30-- young man. And this is my mother. It's a little picture. She was about 52 or so. It was my mother. Yeah, this is my mother-- dear mother. Moisha, whom I lost in the Holocaust. And to his right is William. And that is David, my little brother, who lives here, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right, is my sister, [? Raisi. ?] She is the oldest in the family. She was shoot during the Holocaust time, in Hungary, in the Danube, by the [NON-ENGLISH]. Next, is my sister [? Hamna. ?] She lived in Bucharest. I lef-- I left her four years ago. The last one, to the left, is myself, when I was a young girl, about 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and start, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture from right after the liberation, about three months. You want me to say something else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah ha. This picture was made in Hanover, which is near Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, say, 'these are my children'.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: The right side is my son, with his wife Sharon and two children. The other picture is my daughter with her family-- Leah, her husband, Larry, and the two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Ariel and Diana. And my son's children are Emily and Jacqueline. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] | love them. [LAUGHS] You did [? go ?] [? in? ?]
